title: Stage Intentionally
summary: Use git add to prepare selected files and git diff --staged to verify the exact snapshot before committing.

sketchBlocks.title: The Role of Staging in Git
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Staging in Git is a crucial step in the version control process. It allows you to prepare specific changes for a commit, ensuring that only the desired modifications are included. This selective staging helps maintain a clean and understandable commit history.

Worked example: Suppose you have modified two files, `index.html` and `style.css`. You only want to commit changes from `index.html`. By using `git add index.html`, you stage only this file, leaving `style.css` unstaged. This way, your commit will only include the changes from `index.html`, keeping your history focused and relevant.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspecting Staged Changes
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Once you've staged your changes, it's important to verify them before committing. The `git diff --staged` command allows you to see the differences between the staged snapshot and the last commit.

Worked example: After staging `index.html`, running `git diff --staged` will show you the exact changes that are ready to be committed. This ensures that you are aware of what will be included in your next commit, preventing accidental inclusion of unwanted changes.

sketchBlocks.title: Transitioning Files Between Staged and Unstaged
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Sometimes, you may need to move files between staged and unstaged states without altering their content. This flexibility is crucial for managing your commit preparation effectively.

Worked example: If you have staged `index.html` but decide to make further edits before committing, you can use `git restore --staged index.html` to unstage it. This command moves the file back to the unstaged state, allowing you to continue editing without committing prematurely.

sketchBlocks.title: Applying Staging in Real Projects
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
In real-world projects, staging helps manage complex changes by allowing you to focus on specific parts of your work. This practice leads to more meaningful and manageable commit histories.

Worked example: Imagine working on a project where you need to update documentation and fix a bug simultaneously. By staging only the documentation changes first, you can commit them separately from the bug fix, resulting in a clear and organized history that reflects the distinct purposes of each change.

quizDraft.title: Stage a Specific File
quizDraft.prompt: Stage the file `index.html` without staging any other changes in the workspace. Verify the staged changes using `git diff --staged`.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Stage the file index.html
# Verify the staged changes

# Stage the file index.html
# Verify the staged changes

quizDraft.hint: Use `git add` to stage the specific file and `git diff --staged` to verify.

help.concept: Staging allows you to prepare specific changes for a commit.
help.hint_1: Use `git add <file>` to stage a specific file.

quizDraft.title: Inspect Staged Changes
quizDraft.prompt: After staging `index.html`, inspect the staged changes to ensure only this file is prepared for commit.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git diff --staged` to see the staged changes.

help.concept: Inspecting staged changes helps verify what will be committed.
help.hint_1: Run `git diff --staged` to view staged changes.

quizDraft.title: Unstage a File
quizDraft.prompt: Unstage the file `index.html` after it has been staged, without losing any changes.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git restore --staged` to unstage a file.

help.concept: Unstaging a file moves it back to the working directory without losing changes.
help.hint_2: Ensure the file remains in the working directory.

quizDraft.title: Stage and Commit Documentation
quizDraft.prompt: Stage only the changes in `README.md` and commit them with the message 'Update documentation'.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Stage the README.md file
# Commit with a message

starterFiles.content:
# Stage the README.md file
# Commit with a message

solutionFiles.content:
git add README.md
git commit -m 'Update documentation'

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git add README.md
git commit -m 'Update documentation'
quizDraft.hint: Stage the file and use `git commit` with a message.

help.concept: Staging specific files allows for focused commits.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of Staging
quizDraft.prompt: What is the purpose of staging in Git? Select all that apply.
options: To prepare specific changes for a commit
quizDraft.hint: Consider how staging affects the commit process.

help.concept: Staging allows you to prepare specific changes for a commit.
help.hint_1: Staging helps manage what changes are included in a commit.
help.hint_2: Staging can prevent unwanted changes from being committed.

quizDraft.title: Inspecting Staged Changes
quizDraft.prompt: Which command allows you to inspect staged changes in Git?
quizDraft.hint: Think about the command that shows differences between staged changes and the last commit.

help.concept: Inspecting staged changes helps verify what will be committed.
help.hint_1: The command shows differences between staged changes and the last commit.
help.hint_2: It is similar to `git diff` but for staged changes.

quizDraft.title: Command to Stage a File
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank to stage a file named `index.html`.

help.concept: Staging a file prepares it for commit.
help.hint_2: Specify the file name after `git add`.

quizDraft.title: Unstaging a File
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank to unstage a file named `index.html`.

help.concept: Unstaging a file moves it back to the working directory without losing changes.
help.hint_2: Specify the file name after `git restore --staged`.

quizDraft.title: Staging Area Purpose
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the staging area in Git?
options: To prepare changes for a commit
quizDraft.hint: Consider how the staging area affects commits.

help.concept: The staging area allows you to prepare specific changes for a commit.
help.hint_1: The staging area helps manage what changes are included in a commit.
help.hint_2: It acts as a buffer between the working directory and the commit.

quizDraft.title: Staging Workflow Steps
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the steps in the correct order for staging and committing a file in Git.
quizDraft.hint: Start with staging the file and end with committing it.

help.concept: Staging and committing involve specific steps in Git.
help.hint_1: Begin with adding the file to the staging area.
help.hint_2: End with committing the staged changes.
